meaning: Used to say that doing something is pointless or will not improve the situation.

examples.en: It's no use arguing with him now.
It's no use waiting here any longer.
It's no use worrying about things you can't change.
It's no use calling her again tonight.

meaning: Used to describe what is expected, required, or scheduled.

examples.en: You are supposed to arrive early.
I'm supposed to call her tonight.
We're supposed to wear our name tags at work.
You're supposed to check in online before the flight.

meaning: Used to express a personal preference.

examples.en: I'd rather stay home tonight.
She'd rather wait until tomorrow.
We'd rather take the train than drive.
I'd rather stay home than go out in the rain.

meaning: Used to describe having difficulty doing something.

examples.en: I have trouble sleeping at night.
He has trouble remembering names.
Some learners have trouble understanding fast speech.
I have trouble remembering people's names at parties.

meaning: Used to describe the final result of a situation, often unexpected.

examples.en: We ended up staying late at the office.
She ended up buying the blue one.
I missed the bus and ended up taking a taxi.
We ended up ordering takeout because the restaurant was full.

meaning: Used to show that something is probable.

examples.en: It is likely to rain this afternoon.
He is likely to win the race.
Prices are likely to go up next month.
She's likely to be late because of the traffic.

meaning: Used to emphasize that two qualities or actions are both true.

examples.en: She is not only smart but also kind.
He not only cooks but also cleans.
The app is not only useful but also easy to use.
He not only cooked dinner but also washed the dishes.

meaning: Used to show that as one thing increases, another also increases.

examples.en: The more you read, the more you learn.
The more we wait, the later it gets.
The more you practice speaking, the more confident you become.
The more you practice speaking, the more confident you become.

meaning: Used to say someone is accustomed to something.

examples.en: I'm used to working late.
She is used to cold weather.
He's used to getting up early for work.
I'm used to commuting by train every morning.

meaning: Used to talk about past habits or states that no longer exist.

examples.en: I used to live here.
He used to smoke.
We used to go there every weekend.
I used to play outside every evening after school.

meaning: Used to say something is going to happen very soon.

examples.en: I was about to leave when you called.
She is about to cry.
The movie is about to start.
I'm about to join the meeting now.

pattern: Have no choice but to + V
meaning: Used to show there is only one possible option.

examples.en: I had no choice but to wait.
They had no choice but to cancel the trip.
We had no choice but to move the meeting online.
We had no choice but to cancel the trip.

meaning: Used to describe someone or something having the ability to do something.

examples.en: She is capable of doing the job well.
He is capable of solving it on his own.
The team is capable of handling busy periods.
She's capable of handling difficult customers calmly.

pattern: There is no point in + V-ing
meaning: Used to say that doing something has no purpose or benefit.

examples.en: There is no point in arguing about it.
There is no point in waiting here.
There is no point in calling him again tonight.
There's no point in sending another email today.

meaning: Used to express a condition under which something is true.

examples.en: You can stay as long as you're quiet.
I'll help as long as I can.
You can borrow my car as long as you bring it back by six.
You can borrow my car as long as you fill up the tank.

meaning: Used to describe precautions for a possible future situation.

examples.en: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
Call me in case of trouble.
Bring a jacket in case it gets cold later.
Take an umbrella in case it rains later.

meaning: Used to indicate that something will happen no later than a certain time.

examples.en: By the time I arrived, she had left.
By the time we finish, it will be dark.
By the time you get home, dinner will be ready.
By the time I got to the station, the train had left.

meaning: Used to show that something happens regardless of circumstances.

examples.en: No matter how hard it is, keep going.
No matter how late it gets, call me.
No matter how busy you are, try to get some rest.
No matter how busy I am, I always call my mom.

meaning: Used to describe the amount of time needed to do something.

examples.en: It takes 10 minutes to walk there.
It takes effort to improve.
It takes me about an hour to get ready in the morning.
It takes about ten minutes to walk to the bus stop.

meaning: Used to say someone succeeds in doing something difficult.

examples.en: I managed to finish early.
She managed to pass the test.
We managed to find a parking spot nearby.
She managed to find a cheaper ticket online.

meaning: Used to say an action is valuable or worthwhile.

examples.en: This book is worth reading.
It's worth trying.
That restaurant is worth visiting at least once.
This book is worth reading on the train.

meaning: Used to express being unable to stop oneself from doing something.

examples.en: I can't help laughing when he says that.
She can't help worrying about her children.
He couldn't help smiling when he saw the photo.
I can't help checking my phone during breaks.

meaning: Used to express anticipation and positive expectation.

examples.en: I look forward to meeting you.
She looks forward to traveling this summer.
We're looking forward to hearing from you.
I'm looking forward to seeing everyone this weekend.

meaning: Used to express boredom or annoyance with something repeated.

examples.en: I'm tired of waiting.
He is tired of hearing that excuse.
We're tired of eating takeout every night.
I'm tired of hearing the same excuse.

meaning: Used to show duty or accountability for an action or task.

examples.en: She is responsible for managing the team.
He is responsible for fixing it.
I'm responsible for checking the final report.
She's responsible for organizing the team schedule.

meaning: Used to express fear about doing something or about an outcome.

examples.en: She is afraid of speaking in public.
I'm afraid of making a mistake.
He's afraid of losing his job.
He's afraid of speaking in front of a large group.

meaning: Used to express curiosity or attraction toward an activity or topic.

examples.en: He is interested in learning Spanish.
She is interested in cooking.
I'm interested in joining the class next month.
I'm interested in learning more about Australian culture.

meaning: Used to describe how time is used for an activity.

examples.en: I spend time reading every night.
She spends time practicing speaking.
He spends too much time scrolling on his phone.
I spent the whole afternoon cleaning the kitchen.

meaning: Used to say someone stops another person from doing something.

examples.en: Rain prevented us from leaving.
Nothing can prevent him from trying.
A family emergency prevented her from coming.
The heavy rain prevented us from going for a walk.

meaning: Used to indicate giving permission to do something.

examples.en: They allowed me to enter.
She allowed him to explain.
My boss allowed me to work from home today.
My parents don't allow me to stay out too late.

meaning: Used to show someone is compelled to do something.

examples.en: We were forced to wait outside.
He was forced to apologize.
They were forced to cancel the event because of the storm.
He was forced to take a taxi after missing the last bus.

meaning: Used to express finding something hard to do.

examples.en: I have difficulty understanding strong accents.
She has difficulty sleeping.
Some students have difficulty speaking under pressure.
She has difficulty understanding fast English conversations.

meaning: Used to describe being occupied with an activity.

examples.en: She is busy preparing dinner.
I'm busy finishing work.
Sorry, he's busy talking to a customer right now.
I'm busy preparing dinner right now.

meaning: Used to show using something effectively for personal benefit.

examples.en: Take advantage of the opportunity.
We took advantage of the discount.
You should take advantage of the free trial before it ends.
Let's take advantage of the good weather and go outside.

meaning: Used to express strong enthusiasm to do something.

examples.en: She is eager to learn.
They are eager to start.
My son is eager to show you his drawing.
She's eager to start her new job next week.

meaning: Used to express an impression based on available evidence.

examples.en: It seems that he forgot.
It seems that they agree.
It seems that the train is running late again.
It seems that everyone already knows the news.

meaning: Used to show the final truth about a person, thing, or event.

examples.en: It turned out to be true.
She turned out to be friendly.
The place turned out to be much better than we expected.
The quick fix turned out to be a better solution than expected.

meaning: Used to show the quality or activity for which someone is famous.

examples.en: The city is known for seafood.
He is known for helping others.
That café is known for its good coffee.
That cafe is known for serving great coffee.

meaning: Used to express feeling pleased with something.

examples.en: I'm satisfied with the result.
She is satisfied with her job.
Are you satisfied with the service so far?
I'm satisfied with how the presentation went.

meaning: Used to express knowing and understanding something clearly.

examples.en: Be aware of the risks.
She is aware of the problem.
Please be aware of the time before you start the test.
Are you aware of the parking rules here?

meaning: Used to express having good knowledge of something through experience.

examples.en: I'm familiar with this app.
He is familiar with the process.
Are you familiar with the area around the station?
I'm familiar with this app because I use it every day.

meaning: Used to express purpose.

examples.en: I study hard in order to improve.
She left early in order to catch the train.
He saved money in order to buy a laptop.
I set an alarm in order to wake up earlier.

meaning: Used to compare something to an unreal, imagined, or uncertain situation.

examples.en: He talks as if he knows everything.
She smiled as though nothing had happened.
It looks as if it's going to rain.
He talks as if he owns the place.

meaning: Used to make a polite request.

examples.en: Would you mind opening the window?
Would you mind waiting a minute?
Would you mind helping me carry this box?
Would you mind closing the window for a minute?

meaning: Used to express not having enough resources or willingness to risk something.

examples.en: I can't afford to buy it right now.
We can't afford to waste time.
She can't afford to miss another day of work.
I can't afford to eat out every weekend.

meaning: Used to express readiness or willingness to do something.

examples.en: She is willing to help.
I'm willing to try.
They are willing to adjust the schedule if needed.
She's willing to help if you ask nicely.

meaning: Used to describe what people think should happen.

examples.en: Students are expected to arrive early.
He is expected to finish today.
You're expected to switch off your phone during the meeting.
All staff are expected to attend the training session.

meaning: Used to express the purpose of an action.

examples.en: Speak slowly so that I can understand.
Write it down so that you remember.
I left early so that I wouldn't be late.
I wrote it down so that I wouldn't forget.

meaning: Used to show preference for one choice instead of another.

examples.en: I'd walk rather than wait for another hour.
She stayed home rather than go out in the rain.
He chose to cook at home rather than order takeout.
She chose tea rather than coffee this morning.

meaning: Used to describe participation in an activity or situation.

examples.en: He is involved in planning the event.
She is involved in teaching new staff.
Are you involved in making that decision?
He's involved in planning the company event.

meaning: Used to describe the function or purpose of something.

examples.en: This tool is used for cutting wood.
The room is used for meetings.
This button is used for turning on the device.
This bag is used for carrying gym clothes.

meaning: Used to describe actions that usually happen.

examples.en: People tend to forget names.
He tends to speak fast when he's nervous.
I tend to drink more coffee when I'm busy.
He tends to overthink small problems.

meaning: Used to express ability or possibility.

examples.en: She is able to solve it.
I wasn't able to call yesterday.
Will you be able to join us this evening?
I wasn't able to answer the call earlier.

meaning: Used to express being prepared to do something.

examples.en: I'm ready to leave.
They are ready to start.
Are you ready to order, or do you need a few more minutes?
We're ready to leave whenever you are.

meaning: Used to say something has value.

examples.en: The trip was worth the money.
It's worth the effort.
That course is worth the time if you really want to improve.
This coupon is worth ten dollars.

meaning: Used to describe continuing an action.

examples.en: Keep practicing every day.
He kept asking questions.
Please keep walking until you see the bank on your left.
He kept asking the same question again and again.

meaning: Used to describe continuing the same activity without stopping.

examples.en: She went on talking for another ten minutes.
They went on working despite the noise.
He went on explaining until everyone understood.
She went on talking even after the class ended.

meaning: Used to express desire to do something.

examples.en: I feel like sleeping.
She feels like going for a walk.
Do you feel like eating out tonight?
I feel like staying in tonight.

meaning: Used to express fear about doing a particular action.

examples.en: He is afraid to ask for help.
I'm afraid to say it directly.
Some people are afraid to speak up in meetings.
She's afraid to ask for help at work.

meaning: Used to express a polite desire, wish, or request.

examples.en: I'd like to ask a question.
She'd like to speak to the manager.
We'd like to book a table for two.
I'd like to speak to the manager, please.

meaning: Used to express preference for one action.

examples.en: I'd prefer to stay home tonight.
She'd prefer to wait a little longer.
We'd prefer to sit by the window if possible.
I'd prefer to pay by card.

meaning: Used to describe tiredness caused by an activity or situation.

examples.en: I'm tired from working all day.
She is tired from travel.
He's tired from carrying those heavy bags.
I'm tired from working all day.

meaning: Used to indicate having an opportunity to do something.

examples.en: I had a chance to speak to her.
She had a chance to travel abroad.
Did you get a chance to look at my email?
We had a chance to talk after the meeting.

meaning: Used to show that an action requires time.

examples.en: Take time to rest.
She took time to think before answering.
Please take some time to read the instructions carefully.
Take time to read the instructions carefully.

meaning: Used to show trying hard to do something.

examples.en: Make an effort to speak clearly.
He made an effort to improve his pronunciation.
Try to make an effort to be on time tomorrow.
He's making an effort to speak more clearly.

meaning: Used to describe a repeated personal behavior.

examples.en: She has a habit of arriving early.
He has a habit of checking twice.
I have a habit of making a to-do list every morning.
She has a habit of leaving her keys on the table.

meaning: Used to describe a generally recognized characteristic.

examples.en: He is known to work hard.
She is known to be honest.
That singer is known to write her own songs.
This restaurant is known to get busy at lunchtime.

meaning: Used to express appearance or likelihood.

examples.en: She seems to understand the problem.
He seems to agree with us.
You seem to be in a better mood today.
He seems to understand the problem now.

meaning: Used to describe how something appears to observers.

examples.en: They appear to know each other.
It appears to work fine now.
The store appears to be closed already.
She appeared to enjoy the concert.

pattern: Be supposed to have + past participle
meaning: Used to express that something was expected to happen in the past.

examples.en: He was supposed to have called by now.
She was supposed to have arrived yesterday.
The package was supposed to have been delivered this morning.
The parcel is supposed to have arrived by now.

pattern: Be likely to have + past participle
meaning: Used to show that something probably happened in the past.

examples.en: He is likely to have forgotten.
She is likely to have left already.
They are likely to have missed the last train.
He's likely to have forgotten about the appointment.

meaning: Used to describe something happening by chance.

examples.en: Do you happen to know him?
I happened to see her at the mall.
Do you happen to have a charger I can borrow?
Would you happen to know where the post office is?

meaning: Used to say someone arranges for another person to do a service.

examples.en: I had my hair cut yesterday.
She had the car repaired last week.
I'm going to have my phone screen fixed tomorrow.
I had my watch repaired last week.

meaning: Used to describe intention or design.

examples.en: This button is meant to start the machine.
The room is meant to be quiet.
This message is meant to remind users about payment.
This button is meant to start the machine.

meaning: Used to say someone does not plan to do something.

examples.en: I have no intention of leaving early.
She has no intention of changing her plan.
He has no intention of selling the house.
I have no intention of working late tonight.

pattern: There is a good chance that + clause
meaning: Used to express a strong possibility.

examples.en: There is a good chance that he will agree.
There is a good chance that it will rain tonight.
There's a good chance we'll finish before lunch.
There's a good chance that it'll be sunny tomorrow.

pattern: Be on the verge of + V-ing
meaning: Used to describe being very close to doing something.

examples.en: She was on the verge of crying.
The company is on the verge of collapse.
I was on the verge of calling you when you texted me.
She was on the verge of crying after the call.

pattern: Be in the middle of + noun/V-ing
meaning: Used to describe being currently engaged in something.

examples.en: I'm in the middle of cooking dinner.
She was in the middle of a meeting.
Sorry, I'm in the middle of something right now.
I'm in the middle of cooking dinner.

pattern: Make up one's mind to + V
meaning: Used to express making a firm decision.

examples.en: I made up my mind to quit.
She made up her mind to move abroad.
We've made up our minds to start fresh next year.
I made up my mind to apply for the course.

meaning: Used to describe being stressed or urged to act.

examples.en: He is under pressure to finish on time.
They were under pressure to respond quickly.
Many parents feel under pressure to do everything perfectly.
He's under pressure to finish the report today.

meaning: Used to express having strong justification for an action or belief.

examples.en: You have every reason to be proud.
She had every reason to be upset.
He has every reason to ask for a refund.
You have every reason to be proud of your progress.

meaning: Used to express complete lack of knowledge about something.

examples.en: I have no idea how this works.
She had no idea why he left.
We have no idea what time they'll arrive.
I have no idea why the app keeps crashing.

meaning: Used to express strong dislike or intolerance of an action.

examples.en: I can't stand waiting in long lines.
She can't stand being ignored.
He can't stand people talking during movies.
I can't stand waiting in long lines.

meaning: Used to describe a better or more beneficial situation.

examples.en: You'd be better off staying home today.
We are better off without it.
She's better off taking a short break before deciding.
You're better off booking early to get a cheaper fare.

meaning: Used to describe a habitual inclination toward an action.

examples.en: He has a tendency to overthink.
Prices have a tendency to rise in summer.
I have a tendency to forget things when I'm stressed.
He has a tendency to interrupt people.

meaning: Used to show something is done for a person or purpose.

examples.en: For the sake of your health, get some rest.
They stayed calm for the sake of the children.
Let's keep this simple for the sake of clarity.
She worked overtime for the sake of her family.

meaning: Used to indicate that one thing is done by sacrificing another.

examples.en: He built his career at the expense of his health.
Don't improve speed at the expense of quality.
She shouldn't work extra hours at the expense of family time.
He saved money at the expense of his free time.

meaning: Used to describe having responsibility and control over something.

examples.en: She is in charge of training new staff.
Who is in charge of this project?
I'm in charge of booking the meeting room.
She's in charge of replying to customer emails.

meaning: Used to introduce a thought that comes to mind suddenly.

examples.en: Come to think of it, I haven't seen him today.
Come to think of it, that sounds familiar.
Come to think of it, I still haven't replied to her message.
Come to think of it, I haven't seen him all week.

meaning: Used to clarify that one fact does not imply another.

examples.en: He's quiet, but that doesn't mean he's unfriendly.
I said no, but that doesn't mean never.
Just because it's cheap, that doesn't mean it's bad.
That doesn't mean we should give up.

meaning: Used to clarify that the real reason is different from what may be assumed.

examples.en: It's not that I don't trust you.
It's not that the job is hard.
It's not that I don't want to go; I'm just too tired.
It's not that I don't like the idea; I just need more time.

pattern: The last thing someone wants is + noun/to V
meaning: Used to emphasize what someone least wants.

examples.en: The last thing I want is to argue.
The last thing she needs is more stress.
The last thing we want is to arrive late on the first day.
The last thing I want is to make things harder for you.

pattern: It won't be long before + clause
meaning: Used to say that something will happen soon.

examples.en: It won't be long before we hear back.
It won't be long before summer arrives.
It won't be long before your speaking improves if you keep practicing.
It won't be long before they announce the results.

pattern: There is no doubt that + clause
meaning: Used to express complete certainty.

examples.en: There is no doubt that she is talented.
There is no doubt that this will help.
There is no doubt that he worked very hard for this.
There is no doubt that she worked hard for this.

meaning: Used to show that the result does not change regardless of conditions.

examples.en: I'll go whether or not he comes.
We need to decide whether or not to continue.
Whether or not you agree, we still have to make a decision.
Whether or not it rains, the event will go ahead.

meaning: Used to express impossibility.

examples.en: There is no way I can finish today.
There is no way that was an accident.
There's no way we can get there by five in this traffic.
There's no way I can finish all of this tonight.

meaning: Used to discuss something from a particular aspect.

examples.en: In terms of price, it's reasonable.
The two options differ in terms of quality.
In terms of convenience, this app is better for beginners.
In terms of price, this option is the best one.

meaning: Used to express a personal opinion.

examples.en: From my point of view, we need more time.
From my point of view, the plan is workable.
From my point of view, it's better to start small.
From my point of view, the update is much easier to use.

meaning: Used to introduce an additional negative point.

examples.en: The bus was late, and to make matters worse, it started raining.
He forgot the keys, and to make matters worse, his phone died.
I lost my wallet, and to make matters worse, my card had just expired.
To make matters worse, my phone battery died.

pattern: On the one hand ..., on the other hand ...
meaning: Used to present two contrasting sides of an issue.

examples.en: On the one hand, the job pays well. On the other hand, it's stressful.
On the one hand, I want to travel. On the other hand, I need to save money.
On the one hand, online learning is flexible. On the other hand, it can feel lonely.
On the one hand, working from home saves time; on the other hand, it can feel lonely.

meaning: Used to make a suggestion in a casual and natural way.

examples.en: How about meeting after work?
How about trying the chicken instead?
How about taking a short break?
How about grabbing lunch after class?

meaning: Used to give a friendly suggestion or advice.

examples.en: Why don't you ask your teacher?
Why don't you take a day off?
Why don't you send her a quick message?
Why don't you send her a quick message first?

meaning: Used to ask permission politely.

examples.en: Do you mind if I sit here?
Do you mind if I open the window?
Do you mind if we start a bit later?
Do you mind if I sit here?

meaning: Used to offer, invite, or ask politely.

examples.en: Would you like to join us for lunch?
Would you like to see the menu?
Would you like to try it on?
Would you like to try the new feature?

meaning: Used to talk about plans or intentions.

examples.en: I'm going to call her later.
We're going to move next month.
He's going to study tonight.
I'm going to call them after dinner.

meaning: Used to express necessity or something important to do.

examples.en: I need to charge my phone.
You need to be there by nine.
We need to talk about the budget.
You need to back up your files regularly.

meaning: Used to express obligation or a rule.

examples.en: I have to leave now.
She has to wear a uniform at work.
Do we have to book in advance?
I have to leave early tomorrow.

meaning: Used to say that you want another person to do something.

examples.en: I want you to be honest with me.
She wants me to check the email.
They want us to arrive early.
I want him to check the final version before we send it.

meaning: Used to say that one person makes it easier for another person to do something.

examples.en: Can you help me carry this?
This app helps users learn faster.
He helped me fix the problem.
Can you help me carry these boxes upstairs?

meaning: Used to say that something is excessive and causes a problem.

examples.en: I'm too tired to cook tonight.
The bag is too heavy to carry.
It's too late to call him now.
The soup was too hot to eat right away.

meaning: Used to say that something is sufficient for a purpose.

examples.en: Is the room big enough to fit everyone?
She's old enough to travel alone.
The soup isn't hot enough to eat yet.
This room is quiet enough to study in.

meaning: Used to react to what you hear or to give an impression.

examples.en: It sounds good to me.
That sounds a bit expensive.
It sounds like a great idea.
That sounds useful for beginners.

meaning: Used to raise a concern, possibility, or suggestion.

examples.en: What if it rains tomorrow?
What if they say no?
What if we try a different way?
What if we meet a little earlier?

pattern: The first thing I do is + V
meaning: Used to describe a regular routine simply and clearly.

examples.en: The first thing I do is check my phone.
The first thing she does is make coffee.
The first thing we do is turn on the lights.
The first thing I do is check my messages.

pattern: One of the best things about + noun/V-ing is ...
meaning: Used to highlight a positive point in a natural speaking style.

examples.en: One of the best things about working from home is saving time.
One of the best things about this app is the simple design.
One of the best things about living here is the public transport.
One of the best things about living here is the public transport.

pattern: A good way to + V is to + V
meaning: Used to give practical advice or learning tips.

examples.en: A good way to remember words is to use them in sentences.
A good way to save money is to cook at home.
A good way to improve speaking is to practice out loud every day.
A good way to remember new words is to use them in a sentence.

pattern: The reason why + clause is that + clause
meaning: Used to explain a reason more clearly.

examples.en: The reason why I was late is that the train was delayed.
The reason why she left early is that she wasn't feeling well.
The reason why this works is that it's easy to repeat every day.
The reason why I left early is that I wasn't feeling well.

meaning: Used to add an extra point in conversation.

examples.en: One more thing, don't forget your ID.
One more thing, the shop closes at six.
One more thing, can you send me the file tonight?
One more thing, please bring your ID with you.

meaning: Used to explain the benefit or result of an action.

examples.en: Leave early. That way, you won't get stuck in traffic.
Write it down. That way, you won't forget.
Let's split the bill. That way, it's easier for everyone.
Please write it down. That way, everyone can follow the plan.

pattern: The best way to + V is to + V
meaning: Used to give direct and practical advice.

examples.en: The best way to learn vocabulary is to review it often.
The best way to stay focused is to remove distractions.
The best way to improve pronunciation is to listen and repeat.
The best way to improve your speaking is to practice out loud.
